title: Ethical Transition, Rejection Learning, and Continuing Career Development · Career Campaign Defense
summary: A candidate, trusted adviser, future manager, and privacy-aware transition reviewer. They need a collaborative technical and behavioral defense, access plan, written total-package evaluation, evidence-grounded counterproposal, ethical closure, calibrated rejection learning, and a bounded first-90-days plan. Planned artifact: Deliver Career Launch Offer, Transition, and 90-Day Defense with acceptance evidence, rubric defense, reflection, and a changed-constraint extension. Learners progress thro…
objectives: Defend personal contribution, team decisions, failures, accessibility, security, testing, operations, outcomes, assistance, and non-claims with inspectable evidence.
Prepare learner-controlled requests for accessible scheduling, format, environment, communication, assessment, breaks, or assistive technology.
Compare base, variable pay, equity uncertainty, benefits, leave, hours, commute, equipment, learning, stability, risk, and personal constraints.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# job-search cumulative learner-controlled career campaign
# Browser labs review fictional evidence contracts only. They never contact people,
# submit applications, access accounts, expose personal data, negotiate, or accept offers.
workspace: career
candidate-id: fictional-learner
target-role: [replace with role family, work outcomes, and bounded level]
jurisdiction: [replace with location and current official guidance boundary]
audience: [replace with reviewer need, time, format, and access context]
claim: [replace with one truthful role-relevant behavior claim]
evidence: [replace with revision, artifact, observation, changed case, contribution, and limit]
process-state: [replace with discover, investigate, apply, interview, offer, close, or archive]
accessibility: [replace with keyboard, structure, reflow, status, alternatives, and human review]
privacy-consent: [replace with purpose, minimization, permission, retention, deletion, and safe contact]
decision: [replace with learner-owned next decision and stop rule]
transfer-boundary: learner-approved external actions only
review:

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Project Project Defense
steps.instruction:
project case — A candidate interprets sparse rejection as a skill verdict, retains confidential work, closes relationships poorly, and enters a new role without an onboarding evidence plan. The project variation 6 changes the candidate background, target role, jurisdiction, access need, source date, process state, and decision constraint. Competency probe: Defend personal contribution, team decisions, failures, accessibility, security, testing, operations, outcomes, assistance, and non-claims with inspectable evidence.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Project Project Defense.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for defend personal contribution, team decisions, failures, accessibility, security, testing, operations, outcomes, assistance, and non-claims with inspectable evidence. For Commit to the mission model · Project Project Defense, keep Project Defense connected to mechanically reviewable target-role, truthful-claim, contribution, accessibility, privacy, consent, process-state, changed-candidate, failure, repair, decision, and transfer evidence.

content.text: A candidate interprets sparse rejection as a skill verdict, retains confidential work, closes relationships poorly, and enters a new role without an onboarding evidence plan. The project variation 6 changes the candidate background, target role, jurisdiction, access need, source date, process state, and decision constraint. Competency probe: Defend personal contribution, team decisions, failures, accessibility, security, testing, operations, outcomes, assistance, and non-claims with inspectable evidence.

lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Plan accessible onboarding around relationships, product and user context, systems, quality, security, operating norms, early evidence, feedback, and bounded commitments.

options.text: Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated First Ninety Days condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder. That claim fails the evidence boundary for First Ninety Days.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.

steps.title: Defend the stakeholder delivery · Project First Ninety Days
steps.instruction:
Write the project evidence note for First Ninety Days: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A candidate interprets sparse rejection as a skill verdict, retains confidential work, closes relationships poorly, and enters a new role without an onboarding evidence plan. The project variation 5 changes the candidate background, target role, jurisdiction, access need, source date, process state, and decision constraint. Competency probe: Plan accessible onboarding around relationships, product and user context, systems, quality, security, operating norms, early evidence, feedback, and bounded commitments.

Evidence target: Defend the stakeholder delivery · Project First Ninety Days.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide. For Defend the stakeholder delivery · Project First Ninety Days, keep First Ninety Days connected to mechanically reviewable target-role, truthful-claim, contribution, accessibility, privacy, consent, process-state, changed-candidate, failure, repair, decision, and transfer evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Plan accessible onboarding around relationships, product and user context, systems, quality, security, operating norms, early evidence, feedback, and bounded commitments.

hints: For Defend the stakeholder delivery · Project First Ninety Days, name the learner goal, target role, jurisdiction, audience, truthful claim, contribution, source date, evidence revision, accessibility, privacy, consent, process state, decision owner, non-claim, and transfer boundary, First Ninety Days precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Defend the stakeholder delivery · Project First Ninety Days, trace this concrete input and predict targeting, source, evidence, contribution, access, privacy, consent, process, decision, rejection, repair, follow-up, and learner-controlled transfer evidence before reviewing it: A candidate interprets sparse rejection as a skill verdict, retains confidential work, closes relationships poorly, and enters a new role without an onboarding evidence plan. The project variation 5 changes the candidate background, target role, jurisdiction, access need, source date, process state, and decision constraint. Competency probe: Plan accessible onboarding around relationships, product and user context, systems, quality, security, operating norms, early evidence, feedback, and bounded commitments.

For Defend the stakeholder delivery · Project First Ninety Days, challenge the documented misconception with changed-candidate, target-role, source-date, jurisdiction, audience, access, privacy, consent, process-state, interview, offer, rejection, repair, and learner-controlled transfer evidence: A new hire should prove value by making major changes immediately.
